text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Gerald T. Holloway
DOB: [on file]
Attending Oncologist: [on file]
Department: Hematology/Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT

Mr. Holloway is a 58-year-old male with known stage IVA (pT3pN2aM1a) KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous hepatic metastases, presenting today for routine follow-up visit while on maintenance systemic therapy with 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) plus bevacizumab (Avastin). This visit occurs approximately 7 months from the time of his initial diagnosis and surgical resection. The primary purpose of today's visit is to assess clinical status, review most recent laboratory and tumor marker data, discuss disease status, and address ongoing adverse effects -- in particular, residual peripheral neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin exposure and blood pressure management in the setting of continued anti-VEGF therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Holloway was initially diagnosed approximately 7 months ago with stage IVA sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma. At the time of diagnosis, cross-sectional imaging demonstrated a 4.8 cm sigmoid primary with four synchronous hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm in segment VI, with additional lesions in segments IVb, II, and VII). Molecular profiling confirmed KRAS p.G12D (codon 12), NRAS wild-type, BRAF wild-type (V600E negative), MSS status, TMB-low (6 mut/Mb). He underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy approximately 5 weeks after diagnosis with an R0 resection (pT3pN2a, 3/18 nodes positive, extensive LVI and PNI, tumor budding Bd3). Following surgery, he was seen in medical oncology consultation and commenced first-line FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab with the dual intent of disease control and possible hepatic conversion resectability.

He completed 10 cycles of full FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab before oxaliplatin was discontinued due to cumulative dose-related peripheral sensory neuropathy that progressed to grade 2-3, with significant functional impairment in bilateral hands and feet. At cycle 6 restaging (roughly 3 months into therapy), imaging showed a meaningful partial response in all four hepatic lesions -- the segment VI lesion had shrunk from 3.1 cm to 1.9 cm, with proportional reductions across the other lesions; overall hepatic tumor burden decreased by approximately 39% from baseline. By the cycle 10 restaging, the partial response had deepened further -- segment VI down to 1.5 cm, total target lesion sum down to 3.4 cm (approximately 55% reduction from baseline), with CEA declining from 187 ng/mL at diagnosis to 28 ng/mL and CA 19-9 normalizing to 18 U/mL. Despite this response, the multidisciplinary hepatobiliary surgical team opted at that juncture to continue systemic therapy rather than proceed to hepatic resection, in light of the ongoing neuropathy recovery and to allow for further consolidation of the response.

Following oxaliplatin discontinuation after cycle 10 (approximately 5.5 months from treatment start), the patient was transitioned to maintenance therapy with infusional 5-FU plus bevacizumab (LV5FU2-bevacizumab maintenance), administered on the same 14-day schedule. He has now completed approximately 4 cycles of the maintenance regimen and reports the following:

- Peripheral neuropathy: Gradually improving since stopping oxaliplatin. He reports that the hand symptoms (inability to grip, button shirts) have largely resolved, and he is no longer troubled by the upper extremity manifestations. He continues to have persistent numbness and paresthesias in his feet bilaterally, which he describes as a "persistent sock-like feeling." He is now ambulating without difficulty on flat ground; mild difficulty on very uneven surfaces persists. This is consistent with grade 1-2 transition neuropathy, likely slowly improving over the coming months off oxaliplatin.

- Fatigue: Grade 1, manageable. He reports lower energy than at baseline but is able to perform most activities of daily living and light work from home. He has not returned to full-time active civil engineering site visits.

- Nausea: Minimal on current regimen. He occasionally uses ondansetron 8 mg PRN on pump infusion days but has not required it regularly. Grade 1 nausea at most.

- Mucositis/hand-foot syndrome: No significant mucositis or palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia. The continuous infusion 5-FU component is maintained at the same dose; patient tolerating well.

- Blood pressure: Remained elevated on bevacizumab. Currently managed on dual antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily). Blood pressure readings at home have ranged 140-150/88-92 mmHg, which is slightly above target but improved compared to the more severe elevations seen during full FOLFOX/bev.

- Infusion port: Well-functioning; no port-related complications.

He has not noted any rectal bleeding, abdominal pain, or new GI symptoms. No episodes of hemoptysis, cough, dyspnea, or new neurological symptoms. He denies chest pain. Bowel function: 2-3 formed stools per day, at his new post-colectomy baseline, without diarrhea or urgency. Appetite is diminished but adequate for weight maintenance.

CEA most recently drawn was 22 ng/mL (stable compared to 28 ng/mL at the cycle 10 restaging). No dedicated restaging CT has been performed since the cycle 10 scan; next scheduled imaging will be at approximately 10 months from treatment start (3 months from the last CT) per the monitoring schedule.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue grade 1, no fever, no chills, no unintentional weight loss (weight stable at approximately 181 lbs, BMI ~26).
Neurological: Persistent bilateral foot numbness and paresthesias (grade 1-2, residual from oxaliplatin), improving slowly. No new neurological deficits. No headaches.
Cardiovascular: Hypertension as noted, currently managed on dual therapy. No chest pain, palpitations, or exertional dyspnea. No lower extremity edema.
Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest or on mild exertion. No cough or hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite mildly reduced. Mild intermittent nausea, grade 1. No abdominal pain, distension, rectal bleeding, or change in stool caliber. Bowel function at new colectomy baseline (2-3x/day).
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria, or urinary frequency changes.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain; no joint pain or myalgias beyond general fatigue.
Integumentary: No significant rash, skin breakdown, or hand-foot syndrome.
Endocrine/metabolic: No symptoms of hypo- or hyperglycemia. No thyroid symptoms.
Psychiatric: Reports mild anxiety related to disease status; coping with strong family support. Denies depression. Sleeping 6-7 hours per night.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing, conversant, moderately fatigued male in no acute distress. Appears stated age.
Vital Signs:
  - Blood pressure: 146/90 mmHg (right arm, seated)
  - Heart rate: 72 bpm, regular
  - Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
  - Temperature: 36.8 degrees C
  - SpO2: 98% on room air
  - Weight: 181 lbs (82.1 kg)
  - Height: 5'10" (177.8 cm)
  - BSA: ~2.00 m2
HEENT: No scleral icterus. Mucous membranes moist. No oral ulcerations or mucositis.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Thyroid non-enlarged.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles or wheezes.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Laparoscopic port sites well-healed. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No abdominal masses. Normoactive bowel sounds.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Upper extremity: grip strength intact bilaterally; light touch sensation intact in hands. Lower extremity: mild reduced light touch sensation in bilateral feet (stocking distribution), more pronounced distally; proprioception slightly reduced at toes bilaterally; gait is steady on flat surface, mildly cautious on simulated uneven surface testing. Romberg sign negative. Deep tendon reflexes: 2+ at patellae, 1+ at Achilles bilaterally (slightly diminished, consistent with residual neuropathy).
Port site: Right chest subcutaneous port without overlying erythema, tenderness, or fluctuance. Accessing without difficulty.
Skin: No palmar or plantar erythema or desquamation. No significant rash.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 5,400/uL (normal; ANC 3,200/uL)
  - Hemoglobin: 10.6 g/dL (low; mild normocytic anemia, stable)
  - Hematocrit: 32.1%
  - Platelets: 198,000/uL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L
  - Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
  - Creatinine: 0.98 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2)
  - BUN: 16 mg/dL
  - Glucose: 101 mg/dL
  - Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
  - ALT: 38 U/L (mildly elevated; within 2x ULN -- likely related to hepatic metastatic burden; stable)
  - AST: 41 U/L (mildly elevated; stable)
  - Alkaline phosphatase: 88 U/L (normal)
  - Albumin: 3.6 g/dL

Tumor Markers:
  - CEA: 22 ng/mL (stable from prior 28 ng/mL; down from 187 ng/mL at diagnosis)
  - CA 19-9: not redrawn this visit (last result 18 U/mL at cycle 10 restaging)

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1 (normal). No evidence of coagulopathy.

Urinalysis: No proteinuria (urine protein/creatinine ratio 0.12 -- within acceptable range for bevacizumab continuation). No hematuria.

Thyroid function (baseline monitoring, prior to this visit): TSH 2.1 mU/L (normal).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new imaging available for review at today's visit. The most recently available study is the restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed at approximately 5.5 months from treatment start (post cycle 10 of FOLFOX/bevacizumab). That scan demonstrated ongoing partial response with deepened hepatic tumor reduction -- segment VI lesion measuring 1.5 cm (down from 3.1 cm baseline), segment IVb 1.0 cm, segment II 0.5 cm, segment VII 0.4 cm; no pulmonary metastases or peritoneal disease at that time. Total hepatic target lesion sum had decreased by approximately 55% from baseline.

Next scheduled restaging CT is planned for approximately 3 months from the prior scan (i.e., approximately 10 months from initial treatment start), consistent with standard monitoring intervals for maintenance therapy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. STAGE IVA SIGMOID COLON ADENOCARCINOMA (KRAS G12D, MSS, BRAF wild-type) -- HEPATIC METASTATIC DISEASE, CURRENTLY ON MAINTENANCE THERAPY

Mr. Holloway is a 58-year-old male with stage IV KRAS-mutant MSS colorectal cancer, currently on maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab following completion of 10 cycles of FOLFOX6/bevacizumab. His disease appears to be clinically and biochemically controlled at this juncture -- CEA has plateaued at 22 ng/mL, which, while not normalized, represents an approximately 88% decline from his baseline of 187 ng/mL at diagnosis and is stable compared to the most recent prior value of 28 ng/mL. Mild LFT elevation (ALT 38, AST 41) is noted but not worsening, and likely reflects residual metastatic hepatic involvement at low-volume disease. No new symptoms suggestive of disease progression.

The decision to continue systemic therapy rather than proceed to hepatic resection, despite technically resectable-appearing disease on the cycle 10 CT, was made jointly by the patient, our team, and the hepatobiliary surgery service. The rationale included: (1) ongoing neuropathy recovery, which would complicate post-operative care if major hepatic resection were undertaken now; (2) desire to further consolidate the partial response before committing to surgery, given the multifocal nature of the hepatic disease (4 lesions, including bilobar distribution); (3) pulmonary status was clear on last imaging, making hepatic-only resection reasonable in principle if achievable. We will reassess resectability at the time of next restaging CT.

Plan:
- Continue maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab at current dose levels (leucovorin 400 mg/m2 + 5-FU 400 mg/m2 bolus + 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 46-hour infusion + bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV, every 14 days). No dose modifications indicated at this time.
- Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast to be scheduled at approximately 3 months from last scan (approximately 10 months from initial treatment start). Order placed today.
- CEA and CA 19-9 to be rechecked at next visit.
- Hepatobiliary surgery will be re-consulted at the time of next restaging to re-assess resectability in context of updated imaging.

2. RESIDUAL PERIPHERAL SENSORY NEUROPATHY (OXALIPLATIN-INDUCED, GRADE 1-2 TRANSITIONING)

The patient continues to experience bilateral distal foot numbness/paresthesias consistent with moderate-grade residual oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy, now approximately 6-7 weeks off oxaliplatin. The hand symptoms have largely resolved, which is an encouraging early trajectory. The foot symptoms are expected to continue to gradually improve over the next several months, though some degree of permanent residual neuropathy is possible given the peak grade 2-3 severity achieved.

Plan:
- No pharmacologic intervention initiated for neuropathy at this time (duloxetine discussed; patient prefers to allow natural recovery first).
- Physical therapy referral discussed for balance and proprioception exercises given subtle distal lower extremity proprioceptive changes.
- Safety counseling reinforced: caution on stairs and uneven surfaces, non-slip footwear.
- Will reassess at each visit; if symptoms plateau or worsen, will reconsider duloxetine or other supportive measures.

3. TREATMENT-RELATED HYPERTENSION (BEVACIZUMAB/ANTI-VEGF, GRADE 1-2)

Blood pressure today 146/90 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily. This is not optimally controlled (goal <140/90 mmHg in this context) but is improved from the more severe peaks during full FOLFOX/bev. Bevacizumab-associated hypertension is expected to persist while on continued anti-VEGF therapy.

Plan:
- No medication change today given marginal readings and stable clinical status. Will continue current dual antihypertensive therapy.
- If home BP monitoring reveals persistent readings >150/95 mmHg over the next 2 weeks, patient instructed to contact clinic to consider amlodipine dose escalation to 10 mg or addition of a thiazide diuretic.
- Urine protein/creatinine ratio 0.12 today -- no proteinuria, bevacizumab continuation is safe from renal standpoint.

4. ANEMIA (MILD, NORMOCYTIC -- ANEMIA OF CHRONIC DISEASE / CHRONIC INFLAMMATION)

Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL today, mild normocytic anemia, stable from prior measurements. Asymptomatic except contribution to fatigue. No evidence of active bleeding. Iron studies and B12/folate not re-checked today.

Plan:
- Monitor. No transfusion indicated at current level.
- If Hgb drops below 9.0 g/dL or symptoms worsen, will evaluate for contributing causes and consider erythropoiesis-stimulating agent or transfusion support.

5. GOALS OF CARE AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT

Mr. Holloway has stage IV disease with known metastatic colorectal cancer. Given his KRAS G12D mutation and MSS status, he is not a candidate for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) or immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. The trajectory of his disease and the current maintenance approach were reviewed openly with the patient today. He understands that current therapy is disease-controlling/palliative in intent rather than curative, though hepatic resection remains a potential goal if adequate response is sustained. He is emotionally adjusting well with strong family support; reports mild anxiety which is understandable and appropriate for his situation. He has declined formal referral to a mental health professional at this time but is open to it in the future.

Plan:
- Continue open discussions at each visit regarding disease trajectory and goals.
- Social work consulted previously; patient may reconnect if desired.
- Patient education materials on neuropathy self-management and blood pressure monitoring reinforced.

RETURN VISIT / FOLLOW-UP:

Patient to return to clinic in 3-4 weeks for next treatment cycle and interim labs. Full restaging CT to be scheduled as noted above. Patient instructed to call with any new symptoms including worsening neurological symptoms, significant increase in blood pressure, abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, unexpected weight loss, or new pulmonary symptoms.

Patient expressed understanding of all discussed items and agreed to the outlined plan. Questions answered. Total visit time: approximately 40 minutes.

Signed: [Attending Medical Oncologist]
Dictated and electronically signed on date of service.
